instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
SEO Insertions on What Affects the Premium of Group Term Life Policies? - Article Page
Keyword density and no. of target keywords has been mentioned as comments in the beginning
Suggested additional anchor texts are marked as comments in Yellow. (If required)
Keywords: group term life insurance, group term insurance, premium paying term
Body Content:
Introduction
As the term aptly suggests, group term life insurance is an insurance plan that is designed to give a group of individuals life insurance cover. This plan, in contradiction to an individual or family insurance plan, takes the group as a single unit, into consideration. It is perfect for the requirements that have to be met by businesses, associations, firms, and societies. However, as the group is represented as a single unit, the premium paid is also considered as a single premium for this unit.
Group term life insurance policies are bought by organisations to cover a group of employees. The very nature of any group term life insurance policy affects the amount of premiums paid towards the policy. Typically, employees insured are covered under identical risk. The employer usually pays the premium for the said policy in a lump-sum amount. This means that an equal cover of insurance is given to members of the group of employees under a uniform main policy. For the employer, the benefit of economies of scale is clear, and is more cost-effective than purchasing individual policies. The offer of such policies is also undertaken by employers to attract a talented pool of candidates as this is considered an employee benefit. For employees, on the other hand, this kind of policy ensures security for family and loved ones.
How Premiums are Affected
Premiums can be affected by many conditions and circumstances. Here is a quick summary of how group term insurance premiums can be affected:
The employer has the freedom to add employees to the group term insurance plan. Consequently, this is bound to have an effect on the value of the premium. In case more employees are added to the plan, premiums will increase. However, if the number of employees that are added to the policy equal the amount that leave the organisation or are no longer included in the policy, premiums will not be affected.
The cost of the premium increases when other factors such as employee salaries and age have to be accounted for when the policy is due for renewal.
The employer has the flexibility to alter the insurance cover, and this affects the premium. Under any group term life insurance plan, an employer can opt for additional coverage, like “add-ons” in the form of personal accident, etc. Of course, this is purely subject to a specific limit on the salary of an employee. Although the cost is borne by an employee, the raising of any claim affects the premium amount for the whole group of insured employees.
Group term life insurance plans serve the purpose of covering long-term risks like disability, death, etc. In the event of the untimely demise of any employee, the premium will be affected. There is a greater chance of raising early claims if the number of employees in a group is high. Therefore, whatever the premium paying term may be, premium values can still be affected.
A Case Study
The way that premiums of group term insurance are affected can best be illustrated by a case study. Consider a software company, quite large in size, called “Compubricks IT”. This company has just bought a group term insurance plan for 500 employees who work in this organisation, countrywide.
Vivek is an employee at Compubricks IT and is 37 years of age. He has been working in this organisation for eight years. He is a hard worker and holds a good employee record. Apart from a few leave of absences that he was entitled to get, he has no other record of any absences. However, Vivek has a family history of cardiac disease. Nonetheless, this is a factor that was not considered while Vivek’s employer registered for the group term insurance policy. Now let us examine how the circumstances affecting one employee who is a part of a group term insurance plan can influence the premium value of the plan as a whole.
Vivek has been overworking for the past few months. Engaging in work for long hours and working night shifts (part of his job profile) has taken a toll on his health. Vivek is afflicted with a heart attack, and this takes place on a business trip to the US. Unfortunately, Vivek cannot be saved, and he passes away. According to the conditions of the group term life insurance policy purchased by Vivek’s employer, Compubricks IT has a liability to pay a compensatory amount to Vivek’s nominee as specified in the policy. Since group term insurance is inclusive of global coverage, this is possible. Since one member of the group has passed away now, the premium amount of the whole policy may possibly rise. This is due to the fact that the insurer will consider the present working situation of the given company, and the cost of the claim just raised as a result of the death of an employee.
Conclusion
When you work as an employee of any organisation, you are automatically incorporated in a group term insurance policy as part of the benefits your employer gives you. These plans are advantageous to employers as they cover a group of employees under a single policy. However, they can be beneficial to employees too. They provide employees with tax benefits and security that a certain sum will be paid out should an unfortunate circumstance befall the employee. Nonetheless, as each member of the plan is a part of the whole group, whatever affects one member of the group, has an effect on the premium paid for the group as a whole.

(Context: SEO Insertions on What Affects the Premium of Group Term Life Policies? - Articl...)
